questions.question: Describe two primary responsibilities of an operating system in managing disk drives.
keyPoints: Manage the scheduling of disk I/O requests to minimize seek time and improve efficiency.
Handle bad-block recovery to mark and avoid using defective sectors on the disk.

questions.question: Explain the purpose and mechanics of the elevator (SCAN) disk scheduling algorithm. Include a key advantage it holds over the First-Come-First-Served (FCFS) approach.
keyPoints: The SCAN algorithm moves the disk arm in one direction, servicing all requests until it reaches the end of the disk.
It then reverses direction and services requests encountered on the return sweep.
A key advantage over FCFS is that it reduces the total seek time and prevents starvation by servicing requests in a systematic, sweeping manner.

questions.question: Compare and contrast the performance and reliability characteristics of Redundant Array of Independent Disks (RAID) levels 1, 5, and 10. Your answer should detail how data and parity are distributed, the cost of each level, and its fault tolerance.
keyPoints: RAID 1 (mirroring) provides high reliability by duplicating all data on a second disk; it has a high storage cost (50% efficiency) but excellent read performance and can tolerate the failure of one disk in a mirrored pair.

RAID 5 (block-level striping with distributed parity) stripes data and parity blocks across all disks; it offers good read performance and better storage efficiency than RAID 1 (n-1/n usable space). It can tolerate the failure of a single disk.

RAID 10 (a stripe of mirrors) combines the features of RAID 1 and RAID 0; it provides high I/O performance and fault tolerance by mirroring each pair of striped disks. It can tolerate multiple disk failures, as long as no entire mirrored pair is lost, but has a 50% storage cost like RAID 1.
